title:
Transportation system performance capabilities for vulnerable
populations
text:
1. Introduction 
Transportation systems have a critical role in the functioning of societies as they facilitate the mobility of users and freight, the 
provision of essential services, and the achievement of economic prosperity [1]. However, these systems are under continuous pressure 
to perform with the increasing intensities and frequencies of natural and technical risks. The stress on the capital required tomanage the infrastructure after the occurrence of a disruptive event calls for a shift in the traditional practices of planning and managing 
the resilience of transportation systems [2–4]. Current resilience management practices are primarily focused on ‘engineering resilience’ 
concepts. Engineering resilience conflates resilience and risk perspectives by exclusively expressing resilience in risk terms 
[4,5]. Such practices have led to approaches that focus on mitigating the threats on the physical components of the transportation system; 
rarely including guidelines to reduce the negative consequences on the users of the system, especially socially vulnerable users. 

* Corresponding author. 
E-mail addresses: mha19@fsu.edu, mazinhkarim@gmail.com (M. AbdelMagid). 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijdrr.2023.103991 

Received 13 January 2023; Received in revised form 13 July 2023; Accepted 4 September 2023 


M. AbdelMagid et al. 
Transportation systems are complex social and technical systems that have constant interactions with the surrounding environments, 

e.g. open-loop systems [6]. This dual nature (socio-technical) makes pure engineering approaches weak in producing effective decisions 
related to fostering resilient and sustainable infrastructure systems [7]. Existing resilience guidelines are mostly designed basedon the assumption that individuals can adequately respond to and apply instructions, thereby, failing to account for the fact that aconsiderable percentage of the population can be deemed vulnerable to disasters due to their preexisting demographic and social conditions 
[8–10].
The World Health Organization defines vulnerability as: “the degree to which populations, an individual, or an organization is unable to 
anticipate, cope with, resist and recover from the impacts of disasters” 
[11]. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) define 
vulnerable populations as those who [1] have difficulty communicating [2], have difficulty accessing medical care [2], may need help 
maintaining independence [4], require constant supervision, and [5] may need help accessing transportation. The aforementioned 
characteristics are what define vulnerability in the general context; however, specific guidelines are needed to identify these populations 
in the context of mobility, especially in the case of natural hazards. It is critical to define these aspects of vulnerability becausethey define the needs of the populations that require special attention in transportation planning. Understanding the interactions between 
the social and technical components of the transportation system, especially as it relates to vulnerable populations’ 
needs, is 
important for the efficient and equitable allocation of investments and resources to improve the state of the resilience of the transportation 
system. 

The key motivation behind this research is the repositioning of transportation resilience practices toward the needs of vulnerablepopulations. Vulnerable populations in this research are (i) older adults,(ii) ethnic minority groups, and (iii) rural populations, in the 
State of Florida. The natural hazards affecting vulnerable populations in the study are limited to hurricanes and storm surges. The 
academic goal of this paper is to expand our understanding of the transportation system's resilience from the perspective of vulnerable 
populations. To achieve this, we address three specific research questions. 

• 
What are the social vulnerability characteristics that create vulnerability in the context of mobility? 
• 
What are the shortcomings of the current resilience practices in addressing the vulnerabilities of the focus groups of the study? 
• 
What resilience capabilities of the transportation system contribute to enhancing the system's performance for vulnerable 
populations? 
This paper expands the understanding of the user-system relationships and highlights the shortcomings of current resilience planning 
practices, especially for the benefit of disadvantaged groups. Mixed qualitative-quantitative approaches are used to address the 
research questions, and a hierarchical transportation system performance evaluation (SPE) framework is developed. Using a constructivist 
grounded theory. The set of users' needs and concerns obtained through surveys are transformed into performance capabilitiesto allow transportation agencies to evaluate the system's resilience for the needs of vulnerable populations. We conclude the paper byproposing a framework that offers guidance for transportation agencies to formally incorporate the needs of vulnerable populations intheir documented plans to improve the transportation system's resilience during natural hazards. 

2. Research approach 
This research uses an inductive mixed-methods approach in the development of the system-performance evaluation (SPE) framework 
for the needs of vulnerable populations. The data is collected and analyzed in two main phases. The first phase involved a comprehensive 
literature review to identify the characteristics that lead to vulnerability in the context of mobility. The literature review 
also focused on highlighting the shortcomings of current resilience practices in addressing the needs of vulnerable populations. In the 
second phase, two sets of surveys (vulnerable populations, and transportation experts) were disseminated to assess the perceptions of 
the vulnerable groups and the transportation experts of the users' needs-system's performance relationships. A relative importance index 
was utilized to prioritize the users' needs and evaluate the current state of the system's resilience. The second phase also involvedthe identification of resilience performance capabilities that support the needs of vulnerable populations, and the development of the 
conceptual system-performance (SPE) framework to reflect the different levels of system resilience using a constructivist grounded 
theory (CGT), relying on the information extracted from the literature review and the surveys. Fig. 1 presents the research framework 
followed in the research. 

3. Literature review 
3.1. A critique of current resilience practices for the needs of vulnerable populations 
The term resilience, which originated in the early 20th century [12], has gained prominence through C.S. Holling's definition 
within the ecological systems context [13]. It is evident from extensive literature reviews that resilience is a shared concept across 
various fields, representing the capacity of an element or system to recover stability following a disruption [14]. Holling further categorized 
resilience into engineering and ecological domains [15]. Engineering resilience emphasizes the ability to rebound quickly, endure 
higher stresses, and minimize disturbances [16]. However, current infrastructure resilience management practices primarily focus 
on engineering aspects, neglecting management processes such as planning, preparation, and adaptation, as well as the diverse resilience 
capacities of infrastructure users. Consequently, there is a pressing need for an inclusive approach that bridges engineering 
and social science practices, enabling a comprehensive understanding of resilience in complex infrastructure systems and facilitatingthe development of actionable metrics to inform resilience decisions [17]. 


M. AbdelMagid et al. 
(Image of Fig. 1) 
Fig. 1. Research framework. 

The literature review indicates that the resilience of transportation systems aligns closely with the principles of engineering resilience. 
It emphasizes the ability of a transportation system to uphold its functionality during disruptions and the subsequent effortsneeded to restore its performance level [18–20]. The current resilience management practices that primarily focus on engineering resilience, 
conflates resilience and risk perspectives by exclusively expressing resilience in risk terms [5], treating resilience as a static 
property of the system. This results in frameworks that are only as effective as the predictive capacity of future events [3] and overlook 
the dynamic and emergent nature of challenges facing the infrastructure system. By neglecting the dynamic nature of resilience,
these practices equate system resilience with system stability, disregarding the criticality of adaptation in resilience planning [21,22].

In the context of a rapidly changing environment characterized by heightened unpredictability in recent events, there is a growing 
imperative to recognize the limitations of conventional risk-based planning approaches. The paradigm of relying solely on historicaldata and deterministic models to inform infrastructure development and resilience strategies is proving inadequate in addressing thechallenges posed by uncertain and unforeseen risks. As such, there is a need to embrace alternative strategies that facilitate adaptability 
and flexibility in the face of evolving circumstances. Acknowledging the inherent uncertainties and leveraging approaches that incorporate 
adaptation to account for and respond to dynamic and unpredictable risks becomes paramount for fostering resilience incomplex infrastructure systems [23–25].Recent research efforts have been dedicated to integrating the concepts of adaptation and 
flexibility into resilience practices, specifically in response to wicked problems like black swan events and surprise events [24–28].
These efforts signify an evolutionary paradigm within engineering resilience, aimed at equipping infrastructure systems to effectivelyconfront the emerging challenges that render the traditional practices ineffective in preserving the infrastructure assets. By addressing 
the limitations of traditional risk-based utility maximization approaches in infrastructure resilience planning, these initiatives 
seek to enhance the system's preparedness in the face of deep uncertainties. However, despite these advancements, there has been a 
lack of active efforts to comprehend the diverse response capacities of infrastructure users and their impact on overall system re



M. AbdelMagid et al. 
silience. It is evident that a significant portion of the population is vulnerable to disasters due to preexisting demographic and socialconditions [8–10]. Therefore, inclusive considerations of vulnerable populations in resilience planning are crucial for fostering equitable 
development of infrastructure systems. 

3.2. Transportation systems resilience equity in an age of uncertainty 
A significant limitation of the standard model in infrastructure resilience lies in its failure to consider the inherent variation in sociodemographic 
characteristics among subpopulations and the disproportionate exposure of vulnerable groups to risks. This disparity 
stems from the flawed assumption within the standard model that treats the public as a homogeneous entity [29]. By narrowly focusing 
on the physical performance of the transportation system and treating the impacts of disruptions as uniform, the traditional approach 
inadvertently overlooks critical equity efforts. It disregards the reality that different user groups possess diverse levels of response 
capabilities and available resources, perpetuating an inequitable distribution of resilience efforts [30].

Mounting evidence highlights the disproportionate impact of disasters and infrastructure service disruptions on specific subpopulations 
within communities, underscoring their heightened vulnerability. However, existing research and resilience practices have inadequately 
addressed the sociodemographic factors contributing to these disparities, and there is a lack of data concerning inequity in 
resilience planning [31,32]. While academics and professionals acknowledge this inequity, the recognition often fails to translate into 
concrete efforts, such as policy changes and resource allocations, aimed at promoting equity and the active consideration of the special 
needs of vulnerable populations [33]. Compounding this challenge is the nature of the traditional risk-based planning approaches, 
which rely heavily on data availability to inform deterministic policies for infrastructure management. Consequently, the 
lack of information regarding the societal impacts of infrastructure service disruptions on vulnerable populations discourages decision-
makers from making active efforts to address equity considerations, as evidence from behavioral psychology shows that individuals 
tend to prioritize immediate concerns rather than long-term problems, i.e., hyperbolic discounting [34].

Emerging critiques of resilience practices in academic literature shed light on the inadequacy of the concept in addressing equityissues. Even when equity is considered, it often revolves around the even distribution of resources, overlooking the complexities of 
identities and the diverse needs of different subpopulations [35]. Moving forward, it is imperative for resilience practices to explicitly 
address the aspect of "resilience for whom" and recognize the variations in vulnerabilities and response capabilities among different 
subpopulations [36]. It is crucial for practitioners to develop formal frameworks that incorporate equity considerations into resilience 
plans, as this is a pivotal step in advancing resilience efforts. By acknowledging and actively addressing the challenges faced by vulnerable 
communities, cities can be transformed into resilient environments that cater to the needs of all residents. 

3.3. Vulnerabilities in mobility: understanding the context 
Vulnerability has various definitions depending on the research orientation and perspective [37]. To improve asset management 
practices, it is critical to recognize the variability in the vulnerabilities of the groups exposed to the hazards and develop place-
specific plans accordingly [38]. A review of the vulnerability literature [39–44] indicates that to understand what defines a context-
specific vulnerability (mobility in this research), it is essential to look at social vulnerability beyond the concepts of hazards and risksand expand the consideration to include complex problems related to the issues of residence, resilience, and the resources available 
for the community. Consideration should be given to these factors when there are attempts to determine vulnerable populations in the 
context of mobility (hereafter referred to as mobility-vulnerability), especially those who suffer more negative consequences due to 
natural hazards. 

Residence, as a determinant of mobility-vulnerability, involves considering several variables. These variables include the specific 
physical and environmental hazards associated with a location, the quality of infrastructure in that area and its ability to withstand 
hazards, and the differential access to resources within the community. Analyzing vulnerability based on residence yields varying distributions 
of vulnerable populations, as the choice of variables significantly impacts the results. This observation is supported by research 
combining geophysical risks and social vulnerability indicators in Hillsborough County, Florida [44,45].

Resilience is understood as a construct representing individuals' capability to respond to problems through a hierarchy of response 
mechanisms. It is considered one of the attributes of vulnerability, which measures the level of exposure to hazardous conditions 
[44]. Resilience is measured by the ability to possess and manage various attributes that facilitate effective response to hazards, such 
as efficient utilization of information from emergency communications, effective resource management, and individual capacity for 
proactive decision-making [46]. The absence of resilience in a group increases their vulnerability, even if other attributes are comparable. 
For instance, an individual with a college degree may be less vulnerable than an illiterate person with similar resources due totheir ability to leverage available information and mitigate the impacts of risks. It is important to note that the term "resilience" used 
here differs from the concepts discussed earlier (and later) in the paper. In this section, the focus is on the attributes unique to individuals 
or population groups that contribute to heightened vulnerability but are not directly linked to geophysical threats or resourceavailability. 

Accessibility to resources is an attribute that indicates the level of social resilience of a society; however, some academic opinionsargue that the resources attribute should be kept separate because it can be considered a unique measure for the level of social vulnerability 
which is different from the measures discussed in the resilience subsection above [47–49]. Accessibility to resources is related 
to the ability of an individual/group to respond to hazards, and, unlike the preexisting conditions, the accessibility to resources can be 
managed to reduce the level of vulnerability of vulnerable groups. 

In this research, an examination of sociodemographic characteristics was conducted to understand the variation in mobility vulnerability, 
leading to heightened exposure to risks from natural hazards. These characteristics were derived from the Centers for Disease 
Control and Prevention's (CDC) social vulnerability index (SVI), which categorizes sociodemographic factors into socioeconomic 


M. AbdelMagid et al. 
status, racial/ethnic groups, household composition, and housing style. The SVI's characteristics were specifically analyzed within the 
context of mobility vulnerability, and the findings are summarized in Table 1. By comparing these attributes with those obtained from 
the literature (discussed earlier) four major attributes were identified that contribute to increased levels of mobility vulnerability,
these are outlined in Table 2. 

This study identifies three socially vulnerable groups: ethnic minority populations, rural communities, and elderly individuals (aged 65 
years and older). Ethnic minority groups are considered vulnerable as they often face higher levels of socioeconomic disadvantagesand systemic inequities, and they are usually linked to increased levels of disaster impacts [50]. Rural populations face vulnerability 
stemming from lower quality infrastructure and services, as well as limited emergency response capacities [51]. While the elderly 
population exhibits vulnerability due to their reduced resilience capabilities and coping capacities [52].

We note that focusing on these specific groups is a starting point. Rather than aiming for full inclusivity, this research aims to understand 
the reasons behind the oversight of equity considerations in resilience planning. Through the surveys, the goal is to gain 
comprehensive insights into the needs and concerns of disadvantaged groups. These insights will inform the development of targeted 
strategies and recommendations to promote equitable resilience practices. 

4. Methodology 
4.1. Study context 
The study aimed to investigate the transportation systems resilience for vulnerable populations in the state of Florida by employing 
two sets of surveys: populations' surveys targeting the three vulnerable population groups and an experts’survey targeting professionals 
that work with the needs of vulnerable populations. The objective was to gather empirical data regarding the concerns and 
needs of vulnerable populations during natural hazards (such as hurricanes and storm surges), while also exploring the perception oftransportation professionals regarding the resilience of the system and the concept of equity within their institutions. 

By evaluating the resilience of the transportation system specifically for the needs of vulnerable populations, the study shed light 
on the issue of equitable resilience practices and identified areas where current resilience efforts may be lacking. The collected surveydata was analyzed to develop a comprehensive portfolio of needs that vulnerable populations require from a resilient transportationsystem. In the light of these needs. 

This study represents a practical step towards understanding the gap between resilience and equity planning. While the existing 
literature has highlighted this shortcoming primarily through theoretical discussions, our research offers empirical evidence of this 
disunion. In efforts to bridge the gap between theory and practice, we constructed Transportation System Performance Evaluationand Analysis frameworks that actively consider equity in resilience planning. These frameworks serve as a practical tool to assess and 
enhance the equitable aspects of transportation system resilience, enabling decision-makers to make informed and inclusive planning 
decisions. By generating a preliminary list of equitable resilience capabilities, our study establishes a foundation for integrating equity 
considerations into resilience planning processes and fostering more equitable and inclusive transportation systems. 

4.2. Survey development 
Web-based surveys were designed to collect data and understand the perceptions of both users and experts regarding the resilienceof the transportation system. The surveys followed a similar structure and consisted of four main sections: previous disaster experiences, 
mobility and resilience, economic impacts of hazards, and social impacts of hazards. Descriptions of these survey sections can

be found in Appendix A. For the populations' surveys, an opt-in web-based surveying panel called Pollfish was utilized. Opt-in surveys 
provide a fast and convenient means of data collection, particularly when specific characteristics are required from a large pool of 
participants. While it is acknowledged that opt-in surveys may attract a non-representative set of respondents, empirical evidence 
demonstrates that qualitative conclusions reached are comparable to those of probability-based sampling methods [53]. The target 
subjects for the populations' surveys were residents in areas corresponding to the I-4 corridor, which included counties within the East 
Central Florida Regional Planning Council, Central Florida Regional Planning Council, and Tampa Bay Regional Planning Council. To 
ensure adequate representation of rural populations, rural zip codes in Florida were identified using the definition of rurality provided 
by the Florida Department of Transportation Office of Policy Planning (FDOT OPP). By applying spatial analysis to the Federal 
Office of Rural Health Policy's (FORHP) zip code files and Census Bureau's 2018 American Community Survey (ACS) Zip Code Geography, 
201 rural zip codes and 807 urban zip codes were identified. Fig. 2 depicts the rural areas in the state of Florida. The study focused 
on residents within the Interstate-4 (I-4) corridor for the populations' surveys. 

The target subjects for the expert surveys were professionals associated with organizations or roles in four areas relevant to vulnerable 
population needs: affinity groups, emergency management agencies, transportation offices, and planning agencies. The surveys 
were developed and initially tested with a panel of experts at the Florida Department of Transportation (FDOT) in December 2020. 
Based on their feedback, a final version was developed and deployed in March 2021. 

In terms of response rates, the populations' surveys collected a total of 630 responses (210 from each vulnerable group). Since each 
survey corresponding to the three vulnerable groups (ethnic minority populations, rural communities, and elderly individuals) was conducted 
independently, duplicates were encountered due to overlapping characteristics among respondents (e.g., an elderly individual 
residing in a rural area). Consequently, only unique responses were considered for analysis, resulting in a final dataset of 608 distinct 
samples. In social research, a sample of this size is considered significant, with a precision level of 5%, a confidence level of 95%, and 
P = 0.5 [54]. For the expert survey, out of the 79 individuals who received the survey, 40 participated and completed it, yielding an 
overall response rate of 51%. While it is acknowledged that the sample of experts may not be representative in a strict statistical 
sense, our focus was not on subjective opinions but rather on understanding the existence of plans and measures within institutions. 
Therefore, we determined that a response rate of 51% from the selected professionals would be acceptable to achieve the study's objectives. 
The breakdown of response rates within specific areas of expertise was as follows: 50% within the affinity group, 65.22% 
within emergency management agencies, 58.33% within transportation offices, and 27.27% within planning agencies. The respondent 
pool encompassed a wide range of occupational specializations related to transportation system management, including transportation 
infrastructure management, maintenance, resilience, hazard mitigation, and vulnerable populations. Over 50% of the respondents 
reported having 15 or more years of work experience, with 27.5% having 25 or more years of experience. 

4.3. Measures 
The survey aimed to provide insights about the concerns of both users and experts regarding the performance and state of resilience 
of the transportation system. Respondents were asked to evaluate their level of concern about specific issues related to (i) the 
resilience practices of the experts and (ii) the safety and other resilience-related concerns of the populations. All questions were set 
within a pre-, peri-, and post-hazard timescale to capture different phases of the transportation system's resilience. The main sections 
of the surveys utilized a five-point Likert scale [1–5] for the questions. While the effectiveness of Likert scales has been a topic of debate 
[55], we opted to use this scale to minimize respondent confusion and maximize response rates, as well as to facilitate the handling 
of survey data [56,57]. By employing a standardized Likert scale, we aimed to ensure consistency and comparability in the re(

sponses received from both populations and experts surveys.

4.4. Analysis 
The statistical analysis performed in the study included scoring and benchmarking the concerns of the experts and the vulnerablepopulation groups. Results from the analysis were used assess the current state of resilience of the transportation system as it relatesto the needs of vulnerable populations. This is done by (i) understanding the concerns of the vulnerable populations pre-, during, and 
post-natural hazards (the users' demands), and (ii) understanding the performance and health of the transportation system during natural 
hazards (e.g., the supply of the system). Spearman's rank-order correlation coefficients are used to measure the strength and direction 
of association between the study variables, since the variables in the survey were nonparametric variables [58–61]. The correlation 
coefficients were calculated for both the experts and the vulnerable populations' surveys, focusing on the Mobility & Resilience 
section, to understand if a particular concern is highly correlated to another during the different disaster phases (e.g., is the quality of 
the road network before the disaster related to the safety concerns of the populations during evacuations). To prioritize the concerns 
of the experts and the populations, the relative importance index (RII) was used [62–64]. The relative importance indices for the responses 
were calculated using the formula:

where wi the weighting given to each factor by the respondents (ranging from 1 to 5), A the highest level of concern (weight) possible 
in the scale of the survey question [5], and N the total number of respondents. 

5. Results 
5.1. Experts’ views: transportation systems performance & resilience practices 
The results of the analysis of the experts' survey reveal key insights into the performance of the transportation system, as well as 
equity issues in resilience planning. The analysis focused on two dimensions: physical resilience, which pertains to the resilience of infrastructure 
elements, and organizational resilience, which assesses the capacity of institutions to manage infrastructure resilience. 

Regarding physical resilience, the experts expressed significant concerns about the system's performance during natural hazards,
particularly with regard to supporting infrastructure elements. These elements include drainage systems, power and telecommunication 
infrastructure, and local roads. The experts identified these areas as critical points of vulnerability within the transportation system. 


In terms of organizational resilience, as shown in Fig. 3, the experts highlighted concerns related to the absence of documentationand dedicated resilience plans specifically designed to monitor the system's resilience for vulnerable populations during natural disasters. 
While 62.5% of the experts indicated that their organizations had plans addressing the needs of vulnerable populations, these 
plans were not tailored to the context of natural disasters. However, when considering resilience plans specifically, the percentage fell 
to 22.5%, indicating a lack of comprehensive measures to manage the needs of vulnerable populations post-hazard. The experts expressed 
uncertainty about how to effectively address the challenges faced by these populations in the aftermath of natural disasters. 

The results of the correlation analysis demonstrate a strong association between the absence of resilience plans and concerns regarding 
hazard-related economic and social impacts on vulnerable populations. Specifically, the mean score for economic impacts 
was 4.1 (out of 5), indicating a high level of concern, while the mean score for social impacts was 3.8. These findings emphasize the 
importance of resilience plans in mitigating adverse effects on vulnerable populations.

title:
Achieving Postdisaster Reconstruction Success Based on
Satisfactory Delivery of Client Values within
Contractors’ Services
text:
Introduction
The increasing rate of natural disasters in recent years, caused by
extreme weather events and earthquakes, has led to the loss of
human life and considerable socioeconomic costs (Kim and Choi
2013; Wilkinson et al. 2016), highlighting the need for effective
disaster management. Postdisaster reconstruction plays an important
role in disaster management because it helps restore the livelihood
of affected communities. Reconstruction following a disaster
can be seen as an opportunity to replan the community to
result in better living conditions, for example, through building
improved housing units and infrastructure (Mannakkara and
Wilkinson 2014). In addition, vulnerability for the next disaster
can be mitigated as a result of applying quality postdisaster reconstruction.
However, postdisaster recovery and reconstruction are
considered the least understood aspecsts of disaster management practices (Smith and Wenger 2007; Berke et al. 1993). In the construction
literature, it has been widely accepted that the reconstruction
stage is poorly managed and requires improvement in
disaster management processes (Barenstein and Pittet 2007;
Meding et al. 2011; Barakat 2003; Von Meding 2008; Zhang
et al. 2015).
In comparison to business-as-usual projects, clients during
reconstruction projects in disaster-struck areas deal with greater
risk of failing to achieve their objectives (Ika et al. 2012). One
important reason for this failure is that postdisaster reconstruction
is procured and managed in an ad hoc way (Masurier et al.
2006b) that can lead to slow and expensive reconstruction programs
(Koria 2009). Hence, having appropriate systems in place
in advance is imperative to accelerate the process of recovery after
disaster events through effective reconstruction delivery
(Rotimi et al. 2009). To help achieve this, one essential ingredient
is the presence of good practice within contractual systems into
how to fully achieve client values and satisfaction during a contractual
relationship. In the construction domain, several studies
have attempted to improve delivery of projects based on analyzing
client satisfaction levels within different dimensions of provided
services (Cheng et al. 2006; Soetanto et al. 2001). Project
success and construction quality can be regarded as the fulfillment
of the expectations of those participants, and in particular,
clients as major stakeholders, involved in the contractual relationship
(Barrett 2000; Sanvido et al. 1992). This can be achieved
if the service providers adopt strategies to address client values,
with emphasis on continuous improvement (Barrett 2000).
Hence, it is important to understand how well the contractor is
meeting client expectations. To provide better value to clients,
contractors should recognize clients’ expectations (Ahmed and
Kangari 1995). In this context, the satisfactory delivery of client
values with procured contractor services can be recognized as a
prerequisite to maintaining a contractual relationship and project
success. Literature Review
Knowledge Gap and Current Research Direction
Opportunities can be found for enhancing postdisaster reconstruction
by understanding how the construction sector performs in a
rebuild environment (Wilkinson et al. 2016). It is essential to analyze
how well the available guidelines are implemented in practice
by the reconstruction participants to understand the achievements
and issues from past experiences improve the future disaster reconstruction
projects (Kim and Choi 2013). However, there is a knowledge
gap between the recommendations of the disaster recovery
studies and the associated tools and practices required for disaster
recovery (Eid and El-adaway 2017). From a review of research
trends in disaster management, it can be argued that there is very little
research on global guidelines for best practice in reconstruction
programs after disasters in comparison to business-as-usual projects
(Aliakbarlou et al. 2017c). There are a few guidelines for postdisaster
reconstruction, “but hardly any which are widely endorsed
and can be followed by humanitarian agencies” (Ahmed 2011). The
available literature in terms of disaster response mainly focuses on
developing administrative guidelines to manage postdisaster situations
rather than effective reconstruction services. A few studies
[e.g., Ophiyandri et al. (2013)] were conducted that focused on the
critical success factors in postdisaster reconstructions. Coffey and
Trigunarsyah (2012) emphasized the need to conduct research to
develop critical factors for success in postdisaster reconstruction
projects. “The understanding of what causes [a] postdisaster reconstruction
project to be inefficient is far from comprehensive” (Kim
and Choi 2013). Hence, postdisaster reconstruction service providers
“have not been able to deliver satisfactory reconstruction
projects and this is something that must be remedied as a matter of
urgency” (Von Meding 2008).
To the authors’ knowledge, there is a dearth of literature available
to help understand client satisfaction based on contractual parties’
performance in postdisaster reconstruction. Such information would
help decision makers improve the current state of reconstruction
practices after a disaster. This has been well acknowledged by the
breadth of literature noted in the preceding sections, which are also
discussed in the following sections. The project conditions and management
of postdisaster reconstruction are different from businessas-
usual construction (Masurier et al. 2006a). However, the construction
literature is focused on business-as-usual construction rather
than postdisaster reconstruction. In addition, it is not clear how construction
practices introduced in the business-as-usual literature contribute
to the existing knowledge in postdisaster situations. For
example, much closer synthesis of the existing works in literature is
required that shows the satisfaction levels of clients based on the performance
of contractors in a rebuild environment. Based on such a
synthesis, an argument could then be developed as to how the reconstruction
practices can be improved, which would contribute to and
reinforce existing knowledge of construction practices in a postdisaster
situation. The current study aimed to bridge this gap and
improve postdisaster reconstruction practices based on satisfactory
delivery of client values within contracting services. It assessed how
contractors comply with client values to determine critical values (as
areas of concern in current reconstruction practices) for improvement
to promote postdisaster reconstruction projects. To help achieve this,
the current study identified the levels of importance of the client values
and investigated how contractors comply with these values in a
postdisaster situation. In this study, participants from both public and
private client sectors were involved to compare the levels of public
and private client satisfaction and obtain different perspectives.
Client Values in Postdisaster Reconstruction Projects
Following a disaster, one of the main client requirements is to develop
contractual relationships for reconstruction projects.
Contractual systems include the construction client values within
contracting services, and a service provider who is awarded a
contract needs to comply with these values. Disaster reconstruction
requires a different procurement and management response
than business-as-usual construction because activities and priorities
are modified from the business-as-usual conditions
(Masurier et al. 2006a; Prieto and Whitaker 2011), inducing
changes to the client values within the procured construction
services. To achieve client satisfaction and success in contractual
systems, a concerted effort is needed to identify and satisfy the
client values within contracting services (Ahmed and Kangari
1995). Hence, determining a methodology for addressing client
values and priorities within reconstruction services is an essential
prerequisite for staying organized and focused in a postdisaster
situation. However, there is a lack of research in the theoretical
and empirical study of stakeholder values in the construction domain
(Aliakbarlou et al. 2017a; Zhang and El-Gohary 2016). This
issue is further highlighted in postdisaster reconstruction, which
“is a relatively new topic” (Yi and Yang 2014), and so little time
and attention have been invested in understanding client values
and client satisfaction.
To address the aforementioned need, a recent study by
Aliakbarlou et al. (2017b) developed a conceptual framework
encompassing a number of client values in reconstruction projects.
By conducting a comprehensive literature review, they identified
a number of important values, 140 in total, for clients that
appear to have a bearing on the success of contractual relationships.
For example, postdisaster rebuild programs require a fundamental
rethinking of skill sets to improve reconstruction
processes (Prieto and Whitaker 2011; Rapp 2011; Sun and Xu
2011). Postdisaster reconstruction programs need a better contribution
from the project management discipline (Crawford et al.
2013) to maintain standards in terms of time, cost, and quality
(Mannakkara and Wilkinson 2014). Reconstruction programs
also need to avoid disintegration among contracting parties, develop
effective communication and coordination plan (Hayles
2010), and efficiently comply with health and safety requirements.
Effective planning to deal with uncertainties (Sun and Xu
2011; Hayles 2010; Bosher et al. 2007), complexity (Coffey and
Trigunarsyah 2012; Haigh and Amaratunga 2010), and limited
budgets and resources (Chang et al. 2011; Rapp 2011; Freeman
2004) is essential in postdisaster situations. The client values
identified by Aliakbarlou et al. (2017b) were then categorized
with the nine Project Management Institute (PMI) knowledge
management areas (PMI 2005). Using these values, Aliakbarlou
et al. (2017b) conducted a survey interview with 16 reconstruction
experts from leading organizations involved with reconstruction
programs to define the key client values for postdisaster
reconstruction. Finally, a set of 39 client values was identified as
a result of the interviews. Fig. 1 lists these identified values and
their respective categories. For further detailed descriptions of
the literature review and interviews to identify the client values
mentioned in this section, refer to Aliakbarlou et al. (2017b).
Investigating the satisfaction levels of reconstruction client values
based on the performance of service providers helps to prioritize
the areas of concern as critical values within contracting services.
The research study presented in this article, by employing
the identified values represented in Fig. 1, aimed to identify critical
values for improvement to promote disaster reconstruction
projects. Satisfaction Analysis as a Means to Prompting
Service Delivery
“There is a clear need to significantly improve the reconstruction
process and performance in which the construction industry has a
major role to play” (Zhang and Kumaraswamy 2012). In construction,
client satisfaction determines the bottom line of successful project
implementation (Cheng et al. 2006; Soetanto et al. 2001).
Analyzing client satisfaction will enable service providers to identify
the areas that require improvement and, consequently, better
satisfy clients (Oliver 2014). Hence, satisfaction assessments can be
used as a tool to promote perceived value within construction services
by identifying the strengths and weaknesses of the services.
Analyzing the results of such an assessment helps contractors to
understand what level of performance they need to target to achieve
their clients’ satisfaction.
The concept of client satisfaction assessment is fully developed
in the construction and marketing domain and can be modified and
applied to the reconstruction process. In the marketing literature,
Patterson and Spreng (1997) studied repeat purchasing behavior
and concluded that service value is completely mediated through
satisfaction. Storbacka et al. (1994) supported the use of client satisfaction
as a measure of relationship value. In the construction literature,
Soetanto et al. (2001) and Cheng et al. (2006) applied the satisfaction
concept to improve service quality.
Satisfaction can be determined as a function of a comparison
between an individual’s perception of an outcome and his or her expectation
for that outcome, as suggested by Locke (1970).
Similarly, Churchill and Surprenant (1982) described client satisfaction
within services as a function of prepurchase expectations
and postpurchase service performance. The existence of a relationship
between satisfaction and performance has been supported by
previous studies in the context of performance assessment (Oliver
2014). A study by Cheng et al. (2006) showed that in satisfaction
judgment, performance outcomes are the input, and levels of satisfaction
are the output.
Despite the significance of satisfaction analysis in prompting
service delivery, the review of the available literature shows that the
subject of understanding client satisfaction within reconstruction
services is not well developed and implemented for postdisaster situations.
This study attempted to address this gap. The lessons
learned from this research will contribute to knowledge in the satisfaction
domain for postdisaster reconstruction.
Research Methods
Data Collection
In this study, the methods used to collect data included a literature
review and a questionnaire survey. Reviewing relevant literature
helped in the identification of 39 values influencing client satisfaction
in postdisaster reconstruction, as shown in Fig. 1. The identified
values were then incorporated into the survey questionnaire, using a
Likert scale method, to investigate the levels of importance and performance
of the identified values as perceived by clients. Using this
strategy enabled a true assessment of performance to be undertaken
while also helping in better understanding the levels of importance
of the client values as perceived by different respondents. In adopting
a Likert scale method for a questionnaire with a high number of
questions, using a manageable number of scale measurements for
respondents is essential. In a similar study measuring the quality of
service, a 5-point Likert response format was preferred to other formats
with higher-point scales, as “it would reduce the frustration
level of the respondent and would thereby increase the response rate
and the quality of the responses” (Babakus and Mangold 1992).
Also, because of the purpose of the questionnaire in this study, that
is, understanding the relative significance of values rather understanding
the relationships between them, using the 5-point Likert
scale seemed to be reasonable. In the construction literature, the 5-
point Likert scale has been commonly used for rating the relative significance
of individual factors through examining experts’ opinions
in previous highly cited studies concerning construction performance
[e.g., Aibinu and Odeyinka (2006); Chan and Kumaraswamy
(1996); Lo et al. (2006); Park (2009)]. Hence, in this study, the
respondents were asked to assess the importance and performance of the values (on a scale of 1 –5, where 1 = least significant, 2 = slightly
significant, 3 = significant, 4 = very significant, 5 = most significant).
In designing the survey questionnaire, a pilot survey with seven
respondents was used to collect feedback to improve the final form
of the questionnaire. The participants in the pilot survey came from
a wide variety of construction backgrounds and experience, each of
which contributed some useful dimensions to the study. The objective
of the pilot survey was to analyze how easy it would be for
respondents to interpret and answer the questionnaire. In addition, it
determined the required time to complete the questionnaire. The
pilot study questionnaire was taken as the final empirical questionnaire,
as no adverse comments were received from the pilot survey.
Having considerable knowledge and understanding of postdisaster
reconstruction concepts and extensive work experience in
construction management were the key criteria for selection of the
survey participants. They had, on average, 5 years of experience
with reconstruction projects. They were senior managers from client
organizations and acted as representatives of their organizations.
The participants were also sufficiently qualified to understand what
value meant to clients. They had clear ideas about contractor assessment,
as they were affiliated with organizations that hire contractors.
The list of potential participants was compiled after collecting
the names of high-ranking officials and professionals from various
sources, including institutions’ websites, workshops, conferences,
and the authors’ own networks. In the cover letter of the questionnaire,
the authors informed the participants about the survey background,
objectives of the research, guidelines, and due date. Table 1
presents the participants’ details.
In total, 59 responses, out of 180 distributed questionnaires,
were received, either through face-to-face meetings and interviews
or by e-mail. This equates to a response rate of 33%. Participants
from public and private client organizations were engaged because
the client expectations of the procured services may differ depending
on the type of client (Cheng et al. 2006).
Data Analysis
The data analysis was undertaken through the following methods:
ranking of the level of importance and perceived performance using
the mean score, defining the average satisfaction (AS) and priority
rank (PR) for the values using satisfaction analysis, and identifying
the critical values using the importance-performance analysis (IPA)
method. In addition, Pearson’s correlation was applied to understand
the extent of agreement among participants.
Table 2 has three main columns. The first column includes the
values and their assigned categories. The second and third columns
show the mean score (MS) and ranking for perceived importance
and performance of the value criterion (R), the average satisfaction
(AS), and the allocated priority rank (PR) according to the average
satisfaction gaps, based on public and private clients’ perceptions,
respectively.
Mean Score with 5-Point Scale
Using the mean score method is an appropriate way of analyzing a
questionnaire that includes 5-point Likert scale responses (Chan
and Kumaraswamy 1996; Park 2009). In addition, assessment of
each criterion’s relative significance value (mean score method
with 5-point scale) is a common method for defining the key criteria
among a number of criteria. Park (2009) adopted the mean score
method, using a 5-point scale, to investigate the relative significance
of construction performance indicators. Chan and Kumaraswamy
(1996) used this method with a 5-point scale to analyze the significance
of construction time performance. Lo et al. (2006) used this
approach with the 5-point scale to understand the relative significance
of the causes of construction delays in Hong Kong civil engineering
projects. In this study, this method was adopted to investigate
the relative significance of values in terms of their importance
and performance levels. The criteria were ranked based on their
mean scores (Table 2). The higher the mean score result, the higher
the rank.
The mean score was computed for each criterion using the following
formula (Park 2009):
Mean score Ľ Pđ f  sŢ

where s = score attached to each criterion by the respondents; and
N = total number of respondents for that criterion.
Average Satisfaction (AS) and Priority Rank (PR)
The literature reviewed in the preceding section discussed the significance
of understanding client satisfaction for determination of
success in the construction industry (Cheng et al. 2006; Soetanto
et al. 2001). The analysis of perceived levels of importance and performance,
and the subsequent assessment of levels of client satisfaction,
will address issues for the service providers (Cheng et al.
2006). For instance, it helps contractors to understand what levels
of performance they need to achieve to satisfy their clients. The
analysis of AS adopted in this study has been used by similar previous
studies in business-as-usual construction to improve the construction
service provider’s performance [e.g., Cheng et al. (2006);
Soetanto et al. (2001)]. Average satisfaction (AS) shows the average
discrepancy between importance and performance, as perceived
by respondents, for each value criterion. The difference between average
importance and average performance equates to the perceived
AS by the respondents. For each value criterion, AS is presented on
a scale of –5 toţ5. A positive result indicates the need for improvement,
and the greater the value, the more improvement required.
This computed difference can be used to prioritize the areas requiring
improvement, as explained in the following sections. Fig. 2
graphically presents the AS of the values as perceived by respondents
in this study. The other horizontal line, labeled optimum line,
represents the case where the importance and perceived performance
are the same. Fig. 2 can be used for practical assessment purposes
to represent the perceived levels of client satisfaction within
contracting processes.
Priority rank (PR) ranks the average perceived levels of client
satisfaction of the values adopted by similar past studies
in business-as-usual construction [e.g., Cheng et al. (2006);
Soetanto et al. (2001)]. This ranking represents those values that
exhibit the greatest discrepancy. Hence, they can be prioritized for corrective action, with the contractors required to give more
attention to values with higher PR.
Importance-Performance Analysis (IPA): Identifying
Critical Values
Although AS can help in identifying all values that clients are dissatisfied
with, it can be difficult to fulfill the all these values because
of limited recourses being available in a postdisaster situation.
Importance-performance analysis (IPA) can help in better recognition
of the values that should be prioritized in fulfilling client satisfaction
(Cheng et al. 2006; Chou et al. 2012; Wibowo and Alfen
2014). These values can be recognized as the critical areas for
improvement. Adoption of this method in the current study pertains
to determining the most appropriate use of resources and efforts in
postdisaster situations. The IPA method underlies the multiattribute
models that date back to the late 1970s (Abeka and Abeka 2012;
Martilla and James 1977). The IPA technique is based on the level
of satisfaction of a customer with the characteristics that are derived
from the customer’s expectations and judgment regarding a service
or product’s performance. IPA has been used as a managerial tool
to identify the strengths and the weaknesses of services and products
in various industries (Sohn et al. 2014; Chapman 1993; Cheron
et al. 1989; Abeka and Abeka 2012).
As shown in Fig. 3, the interpretation of the IPA is graphically
presented on a grid and includes two measurements, perceived importance
and perceived performance, thus identifying four areas
(quadrants) for discussion (Martilla and James 1977). Quadrant 1
includes criteria whose level of importance is higher than the average
level of importance, but their perceived performance is lower
than the average performance. Hence, improvement efforts need to
be concentrated in Quadrant 1. Quadrant 2 includes criteria whose
importance and performance are above the average level. The service
provider needs to keep up the good work regarding the criteria
within Quadrant 2. Quadrant 3 is the area of low priority. In
Quadrant 3, both perceived importance and performance are below
the average level. Although the client is not satisfied with the performance,
the service provider may not be overly concerned
because of the level of importance. Limited resources need to be
expended in quadrant 3. Quadrant 4 includes criteria whose level of
importance is lower than the average level of importance, but their
perceived performance is higher than the average performance.
Hence, the service provider may consider the criteria in Quadrant 4
as being overdelivered. These values can provide a competitive
advantage for the service provider.
In addition, the diagonal line in Fig. 3 is the line of optimum and
determines the values that have the same value with respect to importance
and performance. The line of optimum separates Fig. 3
into two areas. The area below the line is the satisfied zone, where
the perceived performance is higher than perceived importance.
The area above the line is the unsatisfied zone, where the perceived
importance is higher them perceived performance.
Pearson’s Correlation
In this study, Pearson’s correlation was adopted to understand the
extent of agreement among public and private participants regarding
the perceived importance and satisfaction levels of the client
values. This method, in the construction literature, has been widely
applied to investigate the extent of agreement among the perceptions
of different individuals (Soetanto et al. 2001; Tang et al. 2006;
Ling 2004; Mir and Pinnington 2014). The size of a correlation
coefficient can range between –1 andţ1. According to Hinkle et al.
(2003), ţ1 indicates a very high positive, –1 indicates a very high
negative correlation, and 0 indicates little if any correlation.
Results and Discussion
In the context of this article, client values are defined as those used
to assess the satisfaction levels of clients based on the performance
of contracting services, and critical client values are those values
that require immediate action for improvement. There are various
client values within client–contractor relationships that can determine
the success or failure of projects, depending on the nature of
the project (Isik et al. 2009). These values are the competencies
required for managing the contractual relationships in any type of
construction project and should be treated as intangible assets in
construction organizations (Zou et al. 2014; Eriksson et al. 2009;
Yasamis et al. 2002). The client values identified in this study can
be divided into terminal values, such as time-, cost-, and qualityrelated
values, and instrumental values, such as criteria concerning
the delivery team’s participants, relationships, coordination, and so
on (Boyd and Chinyio 2008).
Clients have the same objectives, such as procuring a service or
product within reasonable cost, quality, and time (Soetanto and
Proverbs 2002; Hatush and Skitmore 1997). However, the findings
of this study show that clients in postdisaster reconstruction can still
Fig. 3. Importance and performance of values as perceived by respondents
© ASCE be dissatisfied even if their terminal values have been met. This is
because the subject of postdisaster reconstruction success is far
more complicated than just terminal values.
Prioritization of Important Values for Postdisaster
Reconstruction
All 39 client values explored were rated as at least 4 out of 5 by
most of the participants in this study, in terms of their levels of importance.
There was strong agreement between public and private
client participants in this study regarding the importance levels of
the client values, as indicated by the correlation coefficient of 0.770
based on the analysis of Pearson’s correlation (Hinkle et al. 2003).
Among the 39 client values, timeliness, shorter contract time, availability
of resources, delivery speed in the construction process and
shorter lead time, competency, and building trust-based relationship
were ranked among the top 10 important values based on both public
and private clients’ perspectives. The capability of sourcing, the
productivity of resources, maintaining reliability, and a higher
standard of quality were considered among the 10 most important
values by public clients. Budget delivery, financial stability during
the relationship, commitment, and communication technique and
documentation were considered to be among the 10 top important
values by private clients. These values and their prioritization can
be considered in the development of procurement systems for disaster
reconstruction programs. Achievement of client satisfaction
within provided reconstruction services requires some balance and
trade-off among the most significant values.
One of the important recurring themes from this research is that
postdisaster reconstruction clients put the emphasis on instrumental
values when dealing with reconstruction service providers. For
instance, the findings of this study reveal that contract price was not
the dominant value for clients when assessing their contractors. The
findings suggest that in reconstruction contracts, satisfactory delivery
of instrumental values within a contractual relationship is
increasingly recognized as a powerful strategic approach that service
providers use to improve their competitiveness. This indicates a
paradigm shift from focusing on traditional terminal values to both
terminal and instrumental values in postdisaster reconstruction projects.
Hence, time, cost and quality can no longer be the sole and
main determinants of construction contractor services performance,
particularly in postdisaster situations. It is important to emphasize
such values in relation to integration, human resources, communication,
and procurement that are essential for achieving success and
client satisfaction.
Critical Values That Require Immediate Improvement
Developing a management strategy that effectively determines and
helps in fulfilling the client values is important for the success of
construction projects (Rahman and Alzubi 2015; Jang et al. 2003).
This study’s findings show that clients were dissatisfied with contractors’
performance against most of the client values. However,
in reality, it might be extremely difficult to improve the performance
to achieve client satisfaction with all the values. In a postdisaster
reconstruction situation, with limited time and resources,
the contracting parties need to allocate their efforts and resources
wisely and prioritize those aspects that are considered most important.
Determining the expectations for an important attribute
and making a judgment regarding the perceived performance of
that attribute’s performance is critical in attempting to improve
the underperformance.
Fig. 4 shows the client values needing immediate improvement.
Identifying these values can help service providers identify strategies
to improve reconstruction project quality. There was a moderate
correlation (agreement) between public and private client participants
in this study regarding the perceived satisfaction levels of the
client values. The correlation coefficient based on the analysis of
Pearson’s correlation was 0.672, indicating a moderate correlation
between all respondents (Hinkle et al. 2003). The following sections
discuss the top 10 critical client values.
Critical Terminal Values
“A common adage for conventional construction is that the owner
can have a quick schedule, a low budget, and high quality—just not
all three for the same project” (Rapp 2011). These challenges can
Fig. 4. Client values prioritized for improvement as perceived by respondents
© ASCE be more significant in a postdisaster situation. According to McKim
et al. (2000), in reconstruction projects, time, cost, and quality are
highly interrelated, and achievement of efficient project performance
thus requires some balance and trade-off among them. Rapp
(2011) stated that only two of the three can be satisfactorily
achieved. For example, shorter contract time and higher quality are
achievable, but for a higher cost. Lower contract price and high
quality are possible but with a slower schedule. This study identified
six values in relation to client terminal values that require immediate
improvement. Achieving success in postdisaster reconstruction
projects requires a clear understanding of these values, their prioritization,
and particularly their perceived levels of fulfillment in past
projects.
Terminal Values Prioritized for Improvement by Both Public
and Private Clients. As far as business-as-usual construction is
concerned, the contract price is regarded as the most important parameter
in procurement and project success (Hatush and Skitmore
1997; Amandin and Kule 2016). However, this study’s results explicitly
highlighted the significance of values

title:
Developing Resilience in
Disaster Relief Operations Management
through Lean Transformation
text:
Highlights 

o This research aims to investigate the use of lean transformation approach for achieving 
resilience in disaster relief operations (DROs). 
o A systematic lean based method instigated through DROs’ management initiative was 
developed. 
o This was validated through an empirical industrial fire case study where selected lean 
concepts and tools like SIPOC-analysis, value stream maps (VSM), key performance 
indicators, fish-bone diagram, and plan-do-check-act were used to investigate DROs 
resilience in terms of their responsiveness. 
o The VSMs were developed for ‘as-is’ and ‘to-be’ scenarios, and comparative analysis against 
standardized key performance indicators was carried out. 
o The lean transformation approach was found effective in the studied case of industrial fire for 
developing resilience in DROs. 
o Furthermore, lean tools could help in devising pragmatic strategies to prevent delays and 
achieve higher resilience through better coordination, communication, capacity building and 
awareness. 
o This research contributes to the operations management and disaster management fields 
through lean transformation. 


1. Introduction 
Disasters have been affecting social, ecological, financial, and physical infrastructures 
around the globe. According to the Centre for Research on Epidemiology of Disasters, 
Belgium (CRED), 315 natural disasters occurred in 2018 which affected 122 countries 
(based on the EM-DAT International Disaster Database). Moreover, these disasters have 

Hussain, A., Masood, T., Munir, H., Habib, M.S. and Farooq, M.U. (2022). “Developing resilience in disaster relief operations 
management through lean transformation”, Production Planning & Control, in press, accepted 16/11/2021, pp. 1-36, pre-print, 
13/01/2022. Page | 2 


also caused economic damage worth US $131.7 billion, and 11,804 people lost their lives 
while affecting another 68.5 million people. Asia has been the most vulnerable continent 
as 45% of reported disaster incidents, 80% of reported deaths and 76% of the affected 
community belong to this region. Due to increased number of disasters, their severity, 
economic and social impact (CRED 2019; Weru 2015; Below and Wallemacq 2018), 
DROs have drawn the attention of professionals (Masood et al. 2017; Gupta et al. 2016) 
to develop better understanding of disaster resilience. Also it sheds light on designing 
relief activities to reduce human sufferings and damages (Gupta et al. 2016; Bosher 
2014). 

In this regard, Masood et al. (2017) proposed a disaster resilient supply chain 
operations (DROPS) framework as an outcome of an EPSRC Global Challenges Research 
Fund (GCRF) project of the University of Cambridge. As part of this project, the first 
international 5-day DROPS workshop was held at the University of Cambridge (2016) in 
which a mechanism for idea generation, review and control was formulated for 
conducting this research aiming at how resilience could be designed in disaster 
management operations (DMOs), particularly in South Asia. Three follow-on DROPS 
workshops were conducted during 2017-19. Several other follow-on projects were 
completed focussing on various countries. For example, a collaboration-resilience 
(COLRES) framework was proposed for Philippines, which was an adopted version of 
the DROPS framework for the Philippines (Medel et al. 2020). In addition, 46 
collaboration-resilience activities were also identified in this case study of Philippines 
(Medel, Kousar, and Masood 2020). 

Effective and timely execution of DROs is extremely important for saving human 
lives and damages to infrastructure and the economy. There is a need to develop and 
improve upon DROs management strategies preferably based on the real data to strategize 
practices for controlling the damage (Gupta et al. 2016). DROs usually include locating 
wounded persons, moving victims to safe places e.g., refugees camps, hospitals, 
uninterrupted provision of medicine, medical services as these operations are different 
and critical (Masood et al. 2017). The phenomenon of DROs’ management is challenging 
because of the involvement of multiple stakeholders and managing activities in the 
minimum possible time. It is important to consider resilience in the context of DROs 
management as damages due to increase in number of disasters. The resilience in 
disasters’ context deals with adaptive capacity building of resources, active involvement, 
and coordination of all stakeholders at each level of relief processes and collaborative 
exchange of innovative ideas among the stakeholders and social community. 

This research aims to investigate the possibility of using the lean transformation 
approach as a strategy to achieve resilience in DROs’ management. Therefore, the overall 
aim of the research is to explore the overarching question of “how can resilience be 
designed in DROs using lean tools?”. The goal is to formulate a strategized roadmap 
which helps in enhancing the efficiency of DRO and to induce resilience in the system. 
We propose a systematic lean transformation approach for DROs management, which 
includes the use of selected lean tools, like SIPOC Analysis, Key Performance Indicators 
(KPIs), Value Stream Mapping (VSM) and Fish-Bone Diagram, for investigating the role 
of key stakeholders, main causes of delays in DROs, analysing value-added and non-
value-added activities, developing action planned for improvement and then their impact 
by developing ‘to-be’ scenarios through an industrial fire case study. The scope of this 
work is limited to this case study; however, the approach could also be used for 
understanding and managing other disaster scenarios. 

Hussain, A., Masood, T., Munir, H., Habib, M.S. and Farooq, M.U. (2022). “Developing resilience in disaster relief operations 
management through lean transformation”, Production Planning & Control, in press, accepted 16/11/2021, pp. 1-36, pre-print, 
13/01/2022. Page | 3 


The rest of the article is organised as following. Analysis of related literature is 
presented in section 2. The section 3 provides a brief overview of the research method 
being used; Section 4 describes a detailed industrial disaster case study. Sections 5 and 6 
present results and analysis, and discussion respectively. Finally, section 7 frames 
conclusions and future research directions. 

2. Literature Review 
Due to increase in population and urbanization, the effects of disasters are also increasing 
all over the world (Masood et al. 2017; Habib and Sarkar 2017). The single disaster event 
could cause the loss of thousands of human lives and billions of dollars to the national 
and global economy as observed during the Tsunami and earthquake in Japan (2011) 
(Masood et al. 2017; Habib et al. 2019). Such events attracted the attention of the DMOs 
community to research and develop approaches addressing challenges related to such 
events. ‘DROs’ are classified as a subset of disaster operations that are performed after 
the occurrence of a disaster for controlling the impact. (Gupta et al. 2016) presented a 
comprehensive review of disaster operations in Production and Operations Management 
(POM) perspective and recommended the development of case studies with real data. 

On similar terms, DROs management is about managing relief activities 
throughout mitigation, preparedness, response, and recovery to reduce the impact of the 
disaster through better planning and strategies. Humanitarian operations are mostly 
divided into two main categories: disaster response and development programs that are 
performed by government organizations and humanitarian organizations jointly. The 
response phase provides relief supplies effectively that includes food, drinking water, 
medicine etc. to the victims, where constraints like resource scarcity, lack of visibility 
and uncertainty are real challenges (?vgin 2013; Dubey, Gunasekaran et al. 2019; Habib 
et al. 2016; Masood et al. 2017). However, on the other hand, lean concepts and their 
applications have been extensively reported in the literature (Castańeda et al. 2021; 
Hussain et al. 2016; Miller at al. 2011; Wahid et al. 2008). The summary of the literature 
is shown in Table 1.
