title:
MODELLING RESILIENCE OF THE TRANSPORT CRITICAL
INFRASTRUCTURE USING INFLUENCE DIAGRAMS
text:
https://doi.org/10.26552/com.C.2020.1.102-118 

Introduction 

Over the last decades, there is a considerable 
research interest and ongoing discussions on the topic 
of the transportation network (TN) resilience from 
both practitioners’ and academic communities. Several 
approaches to the TN resilience understanding and 
measurement have been proposed and the variety of 
research perspectives including those from the disaster or 
hazard perspective were introduced. The resilience of the 
TN has become an important issue in ensuring operational 
continuity [1] of the TN and in providing of a TN key 
function -to provide means to move people and goods 
between the origin and destination [2-3]. 

Importance of resilience issue increases with severity 
of possible consequences caused by a TN disruption. 
Under certain circumstances, the disruption of the TN can 
cause problems in ensuring the national priorities such as 
economic sustainability [4] and growth, human welfare 
[4], social development, providing security and public 
order [5-6], operational capability of the armed forces 
[5]; it plays a critical role in delivering disaster relief or 
facilitating mass evacuations, but plays an important role 
in disaster management, as well [7-9]. Those examples 
of consequences can be relevant only (1) in cases of the 
large-scale disruptions of a TN or (2) for a small part of 
a dense TN of every state, which can be considered as 
truly important. The second option results in identification 
of the critical infrastructure (CI) notion -a subset of 
the transportation infrastructure that is of the particular 
importance and interest, which is of interest in this article. 

Based on relation of a TN element performance/ 
functionality disruption, caused by a disaster and its 

resilience overcoming, this paper describes the approach to 
the resilience measurement suitable for the transportation 
CI elements. That implies focus on the resilience of TN 
elements in particular, rather than the TN as a whole. There 
are still properties of the systematic approach in form of 
demand change distribution after a disaster occurrence. 
From this reason, but not limited to, a decision-theoretical 
approach, based on Influence Diagrams (IDs), was used 
in this paper. It was used as a tool to model functionality 
disruption of the TN elements after disaster occurrence. 
It is argued that the use of IDs can address two important 
aspects of resilience modelling: (1) provide an improved 
framework for the risk and resilience assessment through 
more elaborate combining probabilities and measures of 
disruption levels after a disaster with respect to not only 
a direct influence (loss of capacity due to disaster) but 
also to often neglected an indirect influence of a disaster 
(change of demand distribution in TN) and (2) facilitate 
knowledge elicitation from human experts through 
a structured approach to the problem. Moreover, the 
approach is complemented by the time decomposition of 
the functionality disruption duration of the TN elements 
with respect to all the relevant resilience features 
(robustness, redundancy, resourcefulness, responsiveness, 
recoverability). 

2 Background and rationale 

The resilience concept originated from ecology [10-11] 
and has been continuously enhanced into other areas 
[12], e.g. business and economics [13], telecommunication 
systems [14], power systems [15-16], production systems 


ISSN 1335-4205 (Print), ISSN 2585-7878 (Online) / © 2020 UNIVERSITY OF ZILINA COMMUNICATIONS 22 (1) 102-118 


and supply chains [17], water distribution systems 
[18], health care systems [19], as well as infrastructure 
systems in general [20]. There are several applications 
and approaches to the resilience assessment in transport 
[21-28] and transportation systems. In technical systems, 
resilience is perceived as an ability of the system to absorb 
disturbance, to tolerate the negative changes of the system, 
while ensuring the basic (essential) functions, structure and 
identity of the system [29]. 

Resilience of the CI is rather new and has been 
intensified especially due to increasing dependency of 
societies on critical infrastructures services [30-31] and in 
terms of disasters, such as Fukushima disaster. According 
to Bruneau et al. [32] and O’Rourke [33], the CIs would 
be resilient if they were characterized by systems that are 
robust (robustness), redundant (redundancy), resourceful 
(resourcefulness) and capable of rapid response 
(responsiveness or rapidity). Based on the research 
approach of different authors and subject under analysis, 
there can be found other features, which characterize 
resilience of a system (e.g. preparedness/preparation [3435], 
recoverability [34-37], absorption [38], adaptation [3738]). 
In definition and description of all the mentioned 
features, an overlapping and similarities in explanation 
can be observed. For the purpose of the paper and better 
understanding of the main features used for the following 
resilience assessment, their definitions are provided: 

• robustness -the inherent strength (also resistance 
[33]), or the ability of elements, systems and other 
measures of analysis to withstand a given level of 
stress or demand without suffering degradation or loss 
of function [33, 39-40], 
• redundancy -the extent to which elements, systems, or 
other measures of analysis exist that are substitutable, 
i.e., capable of satisfying functional requirements in the 
case of disruption, degradation, or loss of functionality 
[15], 
• resourcefulness -the capacity to identify problems, 
establish priorities [39], mobilize needed resources and 
services in emergencies [33, 39] and mobilize resources 
in the process of recovery [39]. 
• responsiveness -the capability of the system to respond 
to changes of an environment and activate the forces 
and means [36] aimed at dealing with the impacts of 
disaster in order to protect an CI element functionality. 
In this article is argued that more resilient system 
response activities should start before a disaster 
occurs (when risk determinants change), followed by 
elimination of the function degradation activities in 
time of the disaster effects. 
• recoverability -responsiveness is continuously 
followed by renewal (recovery) of the element main 
function. It is the ability to recover the desired 
functionality level after a disaster [34, 36]. Some 
authors understood recovery processes as the part 
of responsiveness feature [33, 39], but in opinion of 
these authors the recovery processes differ greatly 
from response activities and therefore they should be 

assessed separately. 

Apparently, all the features can be divided into 
two main groups: (1) features which represent inner 
(structural) ability of the system or element to withstand 
the negative effects of a disaster -robustness, absorption 
and redundancy; (2) features which represent external 
ability of responsible authorities (crisis managers or 
object operators) to protect and recover main function 
of a particular element -preparedness, responsiveness, 
resourcefulness, recoverability, etc. This categorisation 
will be address in the following assessment because it is 
important to consider the fact that a CI element can suffer 
operational disturbance (e.g. due to high snow level) but it 
still can be without structural damage. 

Differences between resilience assessment approaches 
and frameworks mentioned above are basically in 
assessment methodology and final expression of resilience. 
In majority of quantitative methodologies a large number 
of properties are needed [41], which seems to be one of 
the main inconveniences within resilience evaluation that 
should be addressed. From disaster and hazard perspective, 
is very important to capture stochastic nature of disaster 
occurrence and its influence on resilience evaluation. 
Moreover, here is argued that the particularities of a system/ 
subject under analysis (within this paper it is dealt with 
the TN elements and CI elements in particular) should be 
more precisely incorporated into resilience assessment in 
order to propose an appropriate approach for that system/ 
subject assessment. In order to address all of these features 
in resilience assessment, a new modified approach is 
proposed, which is based on Influences Diagrams (IDs). 

3 Methodology -approach to transportation 
element resilience assessment 

Resilience reflects the ability of an element or a system 
to ensure its function(s) in conditions of effects of external 
and internal factors [42]. The effects of a threat can disrupt 
the core function(s) of the system, in this case the transport 
system with core function ensuring population mobility and 
goods movement [3, 5], by overcoming the resilience of the 
element. To what extent the resilience is distorted/disturbed, 
to a corresponding extent the functionality and operability 
of the CI element is disturbed too. This rate of disruption 
is assumed as one of variable for expression of resilience. 
The functionality range from 100% (full performance) 
to 0% (total disruption) is assumed. Moreover, there are 
significant differences in cases of short-term and long-term 
functionality disturbance and therefore it is claimed that 
the time factor (time variable) should be incorporated into 
the resilience assessment. 

Following the mentioned facts, the two main variables 
of the TN element resilience assessment are assumed, 
namely: 

• to what extent the functionality of an element may be 
disrupted (if ever) -Disruption rate assessment,

Figure 1 Example of a TN element disruption profile with incorporating corresponding resilience 
features -conceptual definition 

• how long a disruption may take -Disruption duration 
assessment. 
Several studies have proposed infrastructure 
disruption profile [34-35, 37, 41, 43-44] to capture resilience 
or resilience loss, each with some common (normal 
conditions, disruption state, recovery) and specific issues 
considered. Such a profile seems to be an appropriate 
tool to illustrate and capture the basic relations between 
variables which define a notion of resilience. In order to 
capture relation between here defined variables (disruption 
rate and disruption duration), taking into account all the 
resilience key features, the concept illustrated in Figure 1 
is used as a conceptual definition of resilience notion. This 
concept is closely described in sections 3.2-3.6. 

As shown in Figure 1, resilience is of a continuous 
nature (keeps the same value if nothing happens), so it will 
be more appropriate to evaluate the resilience of the TN 
element by expressing its predicted loss rate. Therefore, the 
loss of the CI resilience can be measured as the expected 
loss in quality of its provided function (Disruption Rate -
DR) over the time (Disruption Duration -DT) [33], simply 
captured in equation: 

Resilience Loss ^ 
, (1)

= fDRDT h, 

The purpose is to produce a numeric measure that 
would quantify the notion of resilience. 

3.1 Disruption rate 
In order to express the Disruption rate of a TN element 
within resilience assessment, it should be clear what will 

be understood as a failure or functionality disruption. 
A disruption of a transportation element can be defined 
in terms of reliability of an element to perform its main 
function [7]. As an important characteristics of the CI 
element to perform its function is considered its capacity 
[9]. The possible loss of that quantity can be considered 
as a failure measure. Basically, the reduction or the loss of 
capacity can be caused by physical (structural) damage or 
physical obstacles on the road. Moreover, there is the second 
performance issue we should take into consideration -
an ability of a TN element to satisfy the transportation 
demand. The rate of satisfaction of that need is assumed 
as the second failure measure. Therefore, the two main 
aspects of the CI elements functionality disruption are 
assumed: 

• direct disruption, which is understood as the impact on 
CI element capacity -Capacity loss, 
• indirect disruption -change in traffic distribution 
resulting in effects on demand on a TN element or the 
TN as whole -Demand change. 
Both impacts have an effect on the main function of 
a TN element and therefore their combination can provide 
overall value for the TN element functionality loss -called 
the Disruption rate. However, while the Capacity loss is 
considered only in a negative perspective (only loss of the 
capacity is considered), Demand change can be considered 
in a positive perspective, as well (a positive effect on the 
TN element functionality) because hazard and disaster 
effects may result also into decreasing demand on the 
TN. In order to capture these different perspectives, we 
assume Influence Diagrams as an appropriate tool to model 
Disruption rate. The modelling procedure is described in 
subsection 3.1.3. 


Table 1 Categorisation of Capacity loss 

Levels of Capacity loss Explanation 
Nominal or no limitation Managing the situation by common means, maintenance and management; comparing the 
maximum construction capacity and the utilization rate of the transportation element; some TN 
elements have a reserve that guarantees the required capacity despite the disaster. 
Low limitation Effect on traffic capacity is low -remaining capacity is able to handle the traffic demands with 
some minor delays resulting from the reduced speed limit, rescue activities, etc. 
Significant limitation Traffic demand is not fully satisfied or it is satisfied with significant delays -there are delays due to 
obstacles (debris) on the transport element, or due to transport route conditions; for some types 
of vehicles (e.g. trucks) the transition can be temporarily disabled -the alternative routes are 
applied (redundancy feature takes place). 
Partial collapse Complete loss of the capacity -due to structural damages (on surface, static, etc.) use of an 
element is forbidden and long-lasting disruption is assumed. 
Total collapse Complete loss of the capacity -in the worst cases destruction of an element without recovery 
option is assumed. 

The Disruption rate is considered as an overall 
failure measure and represent a loss in quality of provided 
function by the TN element. A scale from 0 to 1 (with 
the corresponding transformation into percentage values 
from 0% to 100%) is assumed for the Disruption rate, with 
increasing values indicating higher functionality loss. 

3.1.1 Capacity loss -the direct disruption 
of the functionality of the transport critical 
infrastructure 
There are several reasons causing transportation 
element capacity reduction or loss. They are mainly external 

-disasters, traffic accidents, etc., or internal -incidents, 
maintenance and repairs. The rate of loss is dependent 
on the disaster type, its intensity and severity. The scope 
of present assessment are external triggers, especially 
disasters and man-made incidents (e.g. floods, earthquake 
damages, heavy snow load, wind storm, traffic accident, 
etc.), while the internal factors can also cause long-lasting 
capacity reduction, but the realisation is scheduled (repairs) 
and appropriate measures are put in place to prevent traffic 
complications. The capacity loss rate can be categorised as 
it is shown in Table 1. 
3.1.2 Demand change -the indirect disruption 
of the functionality of the transport critical 
infrastructure 
In addition to the direct impact of a disaster on 
capacity, each hazard can produce demand on the TN 
(cause people to use transportation network). Demand 
on a TN element can be decreased as well as increased 
which also depend on current situation, disaster location, 
the possibility of using both driving directions in case of 
mass evacuation and other TN links availability [6], etc. 
The demand factor, whether increased or decreased, has 
influence on the TN element performance only and causes 
no structural damage to a TN element. If a disaster or 

accident causes a decreased demand on a TN element, it 
could have a positive effect on the resilience rate and vice 
versa. To withstand negative effects of a demand change 
could be a case for more TN elements (due to already rising 
traffic intensity in normal conditions), for the CI elements 
in particular. Therefore, it seems to be a necessary variable 
to be taken into account within the resilience assessment. 
Basically, one understands the ability to manage demand 
change distribution over the TN as the redundancy feature 
of the TN element resilience. For the demand change 
factor the following levels are assumed: Lowered, Normal, 
Increased and Mass Evacuation. Changing demand (if 
one expects any level of increased demand) for a given 
TN element can be addressed by other elements of the 
network, among which the changed demand is distributed 
[45]. In order to ensure replacement of the disrupted 
element, in some cases, the element can be temporarily 
replaced by a makeshift element (e.g. a makeshift bridge); 
usually it would be an element with lower capacity serving 
as a temporary substitution; sufficient for the time being. 
Planning such a measure can increase redundancy of the 
whole TN and an element in particular. 

3.1.3 Modelling disruption rate using influence 
diagrams 
The influence diagram is a probabilistic decision model 
that is a more compact representation of a decision problem 
than a decision tree [46]. The ID captures dependencies 
between variables in the modelled domain, which seems 
to be appropriate to capture relationship between direct 
and indirect disruption of the functionality of the CI 
transportation element. This property allows for more 
compact and efficient (especially in terms of knowledge 
elicitation) representation of the problem, implying that it 
is suitable for larger scale decision problems. 

There are three types of variables (nodes) in ID: (1) 
chance nodes that capture unknown events and relevant 
probabilities including probabilistic dependencies (by 
means of conditional probabilities), (2) utility nodes that

encode utilities (which can be costs, profits, etc.) that 
define user’s preferences over the set of outcomes and (3) 
decision nodes that define elements of the domain over 
which the decision maker has the complete control [47]. 

To model the overall disruption of the CI element 
functionality it is proposed to define a measure which 
capture this quality (Disruption rate). In order to define 
this rate a structured approach, based on IDs, is proposed. 
We assume a fixed three-layer network structure of an ID. 
Nodes that correspond to threats are placed in the top layer. 
The middle layer of the network always includes two nodes, 
corresponding to the direct (Capacity loss) and indirect 
(Demand change) impact on the TN element functionality. 
In the lowest layer a single utility node (Disruption rate) 
would be placed that would combine the effects of Capacity 

loss node and Demand change node. A simple example of 
the ID model is shown in Figure 2. 

A lot of different research approaches related to the 
resilience assessment are based on the subject matter 
experts (SMEs) (represent by relevant stakeholders) 
opinions, consultations, or evaluations [e.g. 48-49]. Involving 
the SMEs into several steps within the IDs modelling is 
assumed. In the first step of modelling, a list of possible 
threats should be produced. This can be done by the 
SMEs familiar with given TN and with the concept of risk 
assessment, or by selection of pre-defined threats, which 
are of particular interest. Threats are assumed to be binary 
(present or absent) and quantified by asking the SMEs to 
provide probability of the threat occurring. 

It is argued, as some authors [50-51], that a system 
might be vulnerable to certain events but be resilient to 


Table 4 An example of definition for the utility node -Disruption rate 

Demand 

Capacity 

Lowered Normal Increased Mass Evacuation 

Nominal 0 0 0.2 0.7 
Low Operability Limitation 0.05 0.25 0.5 0.8 
Significant Operability Limitation 0.5 0.7 0.8 0.9 
Partial Collapse 1 1 1 1 
Total Collapse 1 1 1 1 

others. Therefore, it is important to take into account the 
specific risk and threat profiles to the area under analysis. 
Moreover, the occurrence of multiple events at once is 
highly unlikely, of course, if one omits the possibility of 
a synergic effect; e.g. occurrence of the earthquake and 
flood at the same time is very unlikely, but combination of 
a snowstorm and a traffic accident is very likely. From that 
reason it is assumed that each threat is assessed separately 
and in the relevant cases combination of the two threats is 
assumed. That is also dictated by practical considerations, 
while the burden placed on the SMEs providing probabilities 
should be limited. In particular, nodes with a large number 
of parent nodes (a large number of incoming links) should 
be quantified with a number of exponential probability 
distributions. This implies that if there are 5 parent nodes 
(5 threats) to a node (e.g. Capacity loss node) and all of 
them are binary (have only two states), an SME would be 
required to provide 25 = 32 probability distributions, what 
would be hardly feasible in practice. 

Next step is to designate if the threat has a potential 
to directly affect a TN element, i.e. to block, damage or 
destroy road surface, etc. The SMEs would need to provide 
corresponding probabilities. Similarly, the SMEs would 
need to define if the threat can result in increased (even 
mass evacuation) or decreased traffic -if the demand on 
the TN element can be affected and provide corresponding 
probabilities. The states of the nodes would be as 
mentioned above: Nominal, Low Operability Limitation, 
Significant Operability Limitation, Partial Collapse and 
Total Collapse for the Capacity loss node and Lowered, 
Normal, Increased and Mass Evacuation for the Demand 
change node. Examples of Capacity loss node and Demand 
change node are illustrated in Tables 2 and 3. 

As it is shown in Tables 2 and 3, there are significant 
differences in definition of both nodes. In order to reduce 
the number of parameters required to specify the ID model 
and properly capture relevant interactions between the 
variables, it is decided to set at least the Demand change 
node as the noisy-average node, which is available in GeNIe 
software and allows reducing the number of parameters 
required to quantify the model from exponential to linear 
in the number of parent nodes (the Capacity loss node does 
not meet the required conditions for noisy-average node 
because a threat cannot increased the capacity of the TN 
element, it can only decrease its capacity -see following 
conditions). The noisy-average nodes are suitable for the 
modelled interactions between variables (nodes) where 

the following conditions are met [46]: (1) the parent nodes 
have a state that describes the ‘normal’ state (there is no 
snowstorm, no flood, etc.), (2) the child node has a ‘normal’ 
state, as well (corresponding to typical traffic patterns or 
the level of traffic flow for which the road was designed), 

(3) for the child variable the deviation from the ‘normal’ 
state can be in both increased and decreased values. The 
way the influences are combined is achieved by averaging 
(hence the name), which means that no single parent 
variable is assumed to have stronger influence on the state 
of the child variable than the other parent variables. 
The last step is to combine the direct and indirect 
impact of the threat(s) on the TN element functionality. The 
utility node (Disruption rate) merging the Capacity loss and 
Demand change can be defined as it is shown in Figure 2. 
The probability values are again required to be produced by 
the SMEs (e.g. in Table 4). 

3.1.4 Calculation of the disruption rate 
In order to understand how the approach works, 
an example of the ID model is considered that assumes 
3 different threats: Traffic Accident, Wind Storm and 
Chemical Release. As it is shown in Figure 2, the three 
options are considered by which a threat can affect the 
child nodes (second level/layer): 

• a threat can affect only the capacity of the TN element, 
e.g. a Traffic Accident has a potential to affect capacity 
of the TN element, but it does not produce a demand 
on a system; 
• a threat can affect only the Demand, e.g. an incident 
associated with the release of chemical substance 
(Chemical Release) can result in forcing people to 
leave the threatened area (some form of evacuation) 
and incur heavy load on the TN, but it is assumed that 
the infrastructure is able to withstand such a release 
with no noticeable effects on it; 
• a threat such as Wind Storm can affect both variables, 
Capacity as well as Demand, because there is a chance 
that some transportation links could be blocked or 
damaged due to trees or other obstacles and there is 
still a possible change in demand, in both directions, 
increased if evacuation is needed or decreased if 
people will be afraid of using the TN in such weather 
conditions and at the same time they will feel safe at 
the current position (home, work, etc.). 

Figure 3 Probability of a traffic accident occurrence Figure 4 The instantiated node -traffic accident is present 


a) 


b) 
Figure 5 The ID model for calculation of the Disruption rate for two (present) threats combination 

For each threat corresponding probabilities of 
occurrence are provided: e.g. Traffic Accident: 0.01, 
Wind Storm: 0.001, Chemical Release: 10-5. In order to 
calculate resilience to certain events (threats) the idea 
that some of the events should be considered as “present” 
is implied, rather than to calculate with probability of 
their occurrence. This is dictated by the interest to assess 
the resilience of the TN element “when” a threat occurs 
rather than “if” it occurs. Using of the IDs allow for such 
a dynamic calculation (see example of difference in Figure 
3 -probability of occurrence; and Figure 4 -the instantiated 
node -traffic accident is present) which can be consider 
as the IDs benefit. The same setting option is available for 

the second layer as well, e.g. it is possible to set a state of 
Demand change node to Mass Evacuation or to set a state 
of Capacity loss node to Partial Collapse, etc. 

For the sake of an example, the two cases are analyzed: 

(1) combination of present threats Traffic Accident and 
Wind Storm at the same time (Figure 5a); (2) combination 
of present threats Wind Storm and Chemical Release at the 
same time (Figure 5b).

probabilities, so expected values will vary based on the 
probabilities of the child nodes. The expected utility value

of the Disruption rate node reflects the potential impact 
of an extraordinary event on the functionality of the TN 
element, considering only its robustness and partially 
absorption capacity. It does not reflect other resilience 
features. Therefore these features are included into 
resilience assessment separately and within the Disruption 
duration part. 

3.2 Disruption duration -time decomposition 
of the CI element functionality disruption 
Except for the extent of a TN element disruption, the 
time aspect related to that disruption is necessary to take 
into consideration [52], since with increasing duration of the 
disruption, resilience is lower [34]. Globally, the time aspect 
of a TN element resilience, in this case called the Disruption 
duration, will vary based on resilience features. A broad 
measure of the TN elements resilience which captures 
influence of those resilience key features and their relation 
to a TN element functionality can be expressed in form 
of the time decomposition profile illustrated in Figure 8. 
Basically, there are two resilience features, responsiveness 
and recoverability, that directly express disruption duration 
of a TN element. In most cases, the disruption duration of 
a TN element functionality is the same as the time needed to 
the recover element functionality. Therefore, the recovery 
activities decomposition towards the overall renewal of the 
TN element will be introduced as the most important part 
of Disruption duration. Of course, in cases where there is 
a gradual loss of functionality over disaster impact, we will 
also consider that time as a part of overall resilience loss 
assessment. 

Figure 1 also shows the connection between the 
robustness (partially also redundancy) and the possible 

functionality loss (explained in section 3.1. Disruption 
rate), where the loss of the robustness means the loss of the 
functionality. From the time perspective, the dependencies 
are visible as well. For example, in the worst scenarios 
with expected great disruption of robustness (higher level 
of Disruption rate), the Disruption duration is expected as 
longer lasting and vice versa. This assumes a certain level 
of dependence between the Disruption rate and Disruption 
duration. However, there are two issues to be aware of: 

(1) this logic would only apply to physical damage to 
the element and at the same time the condition that the 
elements being considered are equally large (structurally 
similar, with similar technical conditions) must be met -this 
will guarantee similar robustness; (2) otherwise, in the case 
that only the capacity of an element is affected, the duration 
of the functionality reduction depends primarily on the 
threat, which caused the disruption -significant differences 
in the recovery duration after exposure to various threats 
(traffic accident, snow storm, wind storm, flood, etc.) are 
assumed. It is possible to omit the given conditions -this 
would imply a certain degree of the results distortion, 
but at the same time it will simplify the estimation of the 
Disruption duration. 
For the better understanding of resilience loss profile 
(Figure 6), the following explanations of resilience features 
with related time periods are provided. 

3.3 Responsiveness feature in resilience 
concept t2n-t0 
Responsiveness represents the capability of the system 
to activate the forces and means aimed at restoring the 
function of the system [34, 53] and at prevention of 
a situation deterioration. It is expressed by the time 


(responsiveness period) between the occurrence of an 
emergency, or even before (when the event is likely to 
happen) and the end of degradation elimination process 
and consequently its renewal [36] which can be considered 
as an overlapp
keywords: resilience, disasters, risk reduction, critical infrastructure, transportation, influence diagrams

title: A Review of Resilient Transportation Systems in the Last Five Years
text:
I. INTRODUCTION 

Resilient transportation refers to the ability of a 
transportation system to withstand and recover from 
disruptive events, such as natural disasters, cyber-attacks, or 
accidents, and continue to provide reliable services to users. 
This concept has gained increased attention in recent years, as 
transportation systems have become increasingly complex and 
interconnected, making them more vulnerable to disruptions 
that can have significant economic, social, and environmental 
consequences. 

The concept of resilience was first introduced into the 
scientific community by ecologist Holling[1] in 1973, to 
describe the ability of an ecosystem to recover to a stable state 
under pressure, and has since been gradually introduced into 
other fields such as management and engineering. In different 
fields, resilience has different definitions and evaluation 
methods, but most incorporate multiple aspects such as risk 
management, vulnerability, reliability, robustness, flexibility, 
and survivability. Resilience emphasizes the performance 
degradation and recovery when facing interruptions. A well-
designed system can absorb disturbances and maintain its 
functionality for daily fluctuations. However, for large-scale 
disasters such as earthquakes, the system will inevitably 
deviate from the equilibrium state. The important implication 
of resilience is the system's ability to effectively reduce the 
magnitude and duration of performance deviations from the 
design level. The concept of resilience has applications in 
various fields, but most are based on the same viewpoint that 
resilience is the ability of a system to recover to a normal state 
after changing its state. 

Resilient transportation systems are characterized by their 
ability to absorb and adapt to disruptions, maintain essential 
services, and recover quickly from disruptions. To achieve 
this, such systems need to be designed, operated, and 
maintained with resilience in mind, which involves several 
key components, including redundancy, diversity, flexibility, 
and adaptability. 

Redundancy refers to the duplication of critical 
transportation infrastructure, such as roads, bridges, and 
tunnels, to ensure that alternative routes are available in case 
of disruptions. Diversity involves designing a transportation 
system that offers multiple modes of transportation, such as 
buses, trains, and bicycles, to reduce the dependence on a 
single mode and provide more options to users. Flexibility 
entails designing a transportation system that can quickly 
adapt to changing circumstances, such as rerouting traffic 
during a disaster, or changing transit schedules to meet 
demand. Finally, adaptability involves designing a 
transportation system that can learn from past disruptions and 
improve its resilience over time. 

The article is a comprehensive review of the concepts and 
methodologies of resilient transportation that have been 
extensively studied in transportation systems over the past five 
years. The paper discusses the metrics and mathematical 
models used in the selected core papers to measure resilience 
and the strategies used to improve resilience. Gaps and 
challenges in the reviewed methods are discussed, several 
popular subtopics are identified and discussed and future 
research directions are suggested. This paper aims to provide 


an updated and comprehensive review of the concepts and 
methodologies related to resilient transportation, building on 
previous research in the field to expand on the prior work. 

The remainder of this paper is organized as the followings. 
In Section II, details of the literature related to resilient 
transport systems are provided, including a review of the 
metrics and mathematical models used to measure resilience 
and strategies for enhancing resilience are discussed. In 
Section III, several popular subtopics in the literature are 
identified. Finally, Section IV gives the future research 
direction. 

II. LITERATURE REVIEW 

In this part, a review of related papers is provided. The 
metrics and mathematical models used to measure resilience 
and strategies used to enhance resilience are reviewed 
respectively. The literature search utilizes the core database of 
Web of Science, and the publication time of the literature is 
limited to the past five years. The keywords cover resilient 
transportation system, resilience assessment, resilience 
indicator, resilient transportation infrastructure, climate 
resilience in transportation, resilient transportation policies 
and strategies, and risk management in transportation systems. 
We restrict the scope to academic papers in English, including 
journal papers and conference proceedings from 2019 to 2023, 
as well as a small amount of literature published in 2018. After 
deleting the reduplicative papers, overall 1062 papers are 
selected. After this step, we further conduct a manual selection 
based on abstract review, we find a large number of irrelevant 
papers. Finally, we eliminate papers that solely mention 
resilience in the title, abstract, or keywords without providing 
substantive discussions in the main body. As a result, we 
identify 61 papers. When we thoroughly examine these 61 
articles, we discover that several significant citations, which 
were initially overlooked in the aforementioned search, are 
included. As a result, the core set of papers comprises a total 
of 65 articles. In order to identify existing issues and future 
gaps, the literature is explained in detail. 

A. Resilience Metrics 

The process of defining metrics plays a crucial role in the 
development of measurement tools. This section presents a 
number of metrics used in the relevant literature over the past 
five years. The metrics for measuring the resilience of 
transportation systems can be categorized into three main 
categories: topological metrics, attributes-based metrics, and 
performance-based metrics. Topological metrics rely on the 
analysis of structural properties, such as betweenness 
centrality or shortest path length. Attributes-based metrics and 
performance-based metrics take into account both the system's 
structure and the flow of traffic within it. The distinction 
between attributes-based and performance-based metrics lies 
in the fact that the former assess resilience from specific 
aspects, such as recovery speed, while the latter are designed 
to provide a comprehensive evaluation of the system's 
resilience. 

Topological metrics primarily focus on the structural 
aspects of transportation systems while disregarding dynamic 
features. Transportation infrastructure consists of many 
networks, such as roads and railroads, and graph theory is 
often employed to measure topological characteristics. These 
metrics are commonly derived from graph-based properties, 
such as the size of a giant component, average shortest paths, 
betweenness centrality, and connectivity. Aydin et al. 
(2018)[2] developed a framework using the Giant Connected 
Component (GCC) as a common metric to assess recovery 
strategies in transportation networks. Their methodology was 
applied to a case study in Nepal, providing insights into road 
network recovery and aiding decision-making for resilience 
enhancement. Amini et al. (2018)[3] proposed a methodology 
that utilizes the macroscopic fundamental diagram (MFD) to 
evaluate road network performance during non-recurring 
congestion. The proposed indicator is obtained by calculating 
the weighted space-mean flow of an urban network. Ilbeigi 
(2019)[4] used taxi GPS traces to analyze the resilience of 
transportation networks, utilizing closeness centrality as a 
measure of network connectivity. The study demonstrated the 
approach's application in assessing New York City's 
transportation network resilience during Hurricane Sandy, 
aiding decision-makers in identifying vulnerabilities and 
enhancing resilience planning. Wu et al. (2019)[5] developed 
a framework to assess the resilience of maritime shipping 
networks by considering the decline in network efficiency and 
the size of the largest connected subgraph after port failure. 
These indicators reflect the vulnerability of the shipping 
network to transportation system disruptions. 
Tachaudomdach et al. (2021)[6] introduced a method to 
quantify resilience indicators using edge- and node-
betweenness centrality models. They assessed the damaged 
area, number of affected links, number of affected nodes, and 
number of affected cars as indicators of resilience for different 
levels of floods. Abenayake et al. (2022)[7] conducted a study 
on the impact of urban flooding on transportation systems and 
human activity patterns. They analyzed the topological 
characteristics of the transportation network using proximity 
centrality (CC) and betweenness centrality (BC) metrics to 
identify critical parts of the road during flood events. Qi et al. 
(2022)[8] explored node centralities and topological 
indicators to assess the resilience of urban metro networks. 
They found that tunnel failure had the least impact on the 
network, followed by general station interruption, while 
interruption at interchange stations had the highest 
consequences. Lee et al. (2022)[9] conducted a study to assess 
the criticality of road segments based on their resilience 
performance. The study utilized four quantitative metrics, 
including the connectivity of road segments, vulnerability to 
flooding, proximity to essential facilities, and exposure to 
natural disasters. The study proposed two integrated metrics 
that combined all the four metrics to show the overall 
resilience performance of road segments. Serdar and Al-
Ghamdi's (2022)[10] study on the resiliency assessment of 
road networks during the FIFA World Cup Qatar 2022 used 
betweenness as a performance indicator. This metric measures 
a node's role in connecting other nodes in the network by 
calculating the fraction of shortest paths passing through the 
node and connecting pairs of other nodes. 

Attribute-based metrics are commonly used to measure the 
resilience of transportation systems, focusing on specific 
attributes such as robustness, redundancy, resourcefulness, 
and rapidity. Attribute-based metrics aim to assess the 
system's resilience in terms of performance over a specific 
period by considering one or a few of these attributes. Liao et 
al. (2018)[11] developed a resource optimal model to assess 
the resilience of transportation networks under disaster 
situations. They measured three performance indicators, 
namely coping capacity, robustness, and flexibility, to 
evaluate the network's performance. Rahimi-Golkhandan et al. 


(2019)[12] adapted ecological diversity metrics of richness 
and evenness to develop an approach to characterize 
transportation system diversity using ecological concepts. 
They applied this approach to New York City and identified 
varying levels of diversity at the zip code level. Tang et al. 
(2019)[13] analyzed the general resilience of Beijing's road 
transportation system by adding an indicator layer below the 
quality layer. The indicators, chosen from road system 
performance, included measures of serviceability, robustness, 
safety, repairability, affordability, and adaptability. These 
indicators were used to calculate the resilience of the system, 
considering factors such as road length, economic losses, 
traffic incidents, road personnel, transportation expenses, and 
transportation patents. The Resilience Triangle (Bruneau et al., 
2003)[14] is a classic method to quantify the key features of 
resilience evaluation. Wang et al. (2020)[15] extended the 
idea of the Resilience Triangle and proposed resilience indices 
based on traffic speed fluctuation and precipitation correlation 
to measure transportation resilience. They introduced five 
indices, including Loss of Resilience, Response Time, 
Recovery Time, Response Rate, and Recovery Rate. The 
study utilized real-world big data and developed an end-to-end 
deep learning framework to estimate and predict 
transportation resilience under extreme weather events. Zhou 
et al. (2021)[16] proposed a method for quantitatively 
assessing the impacts of typhoons on road networks using 
recovery time as a metric. The study utilized meteorological 
warning signals and a resilience curve to evaluate the 
resilience of road networks in the face of typhoons. Wei et al. 
(2023)[17] developed a model to assess the resilience of 
subway lines considering factors such as line characteristics, 
passenger flow, capacity, and emergency response capacity. 
They used Bayesian network and FMEA to integrate multiple 
performance indicators and calculate the weighted average for 
overall resilience assessment. The approach enables real-time 
assessment of the subway system's resilience during extreme 
weather events. 

Performance-based metrics are designed to measure the 
resilience of road infrastructure based on their actual 
performance during and after disasters. These metrics focus 
on the service level, capacity, and travel time of road segments, 
providing a more accurate and realistic picture of the actual 
resilience of road networks. Li and Rong (2019)[18] 
developed performance-based indexes for the Air 
Transportation Network (ATN) using the shortest travel time 
as a measure. These indexes assessed the accessibility 
efficiency between airports and reflected the overall resilience 
of the network. The study by Wang et al. (2019)[19] analyzed 
the operational resilience of air transport networks, proposing 
four metrics to assess the performance of the air transport 
system, including airport departure delay, System-Wide Delay, 
punctuality, and General Resilience Index. These metrics are 
commonly used in resilience studies of transportation systems 
to quantify their performance during and after disruptions. 
Kaviani et al. (2020)[20] presented an optimization model for 
recovery planning of road networks, considering both demand 
satisfaction (D) and travel time (TT) resilience for people and 
goods movement. They proposed two indicators to assess the 
resilience of the network: one measured the demand 
satisfaction resilience against a disruptive event, and the other 
focused on the travel time resilience. Zhang et al. (2020)[21] 
evaluated the resilience of urban rail transit using dynamic 
resilience metrics that assessed the system's performance over 
time, considering factors such as waiting time caused by 
disruptions and the line's transport capacity. A higher 
resilience index indicated better performance and the ability 
to quickly recover and adapt to disruptions or changes. 
Twumasi-Boakye and Sobanjo (2021)[22] proposed a 
resilience metric for seismic resilience studies, considering 
network performance loss and recovery time. The metric 
accounted for the performance loss from a disaster occurrence 
to full system recovery, emphasizing the significance of rapid 
recovery and the reduction of failure probabilities and 
consequences in transportation networks. Wang et al. 
(2022)[23] studied the resilience of airport flight operations 
under severe weather conditions. They proposed departure 
punctuality (DPP) and departure rate (DPR) as indicators to 
measure the resilience of airport flight performance. The study 
assessed system capacities of susceptibility, absorption, 
adaptation, and recovery throughout five phases before and 
after a disturbance. Chen et al. (2022)[24] introduced a 
comprehensive approach to assess road performance by 
combining multi-dimensional indexes, including topological, 
attribute-based, and performance-based indicators. This 
integrated methodology provided a more comprehensive 
understanding of road conditions and enabled effective 
decision-making for road management and improvement. 
Rahdar et al. (2022)[25] proposed a trilevel optimization 
model to enhance the resilience of a transportation network 
considering uncertainties. The model minimized total travel 
time by designing a resilient network that considered 
uncertain disruptions and deterministic origin-destination 
demands. It considered both System Optimal (SO) and User 
Equilibrium (UE) shortest total travel time to optimize 
network performance. 

Performance-based metrics are considered more effective 
in measuring transportation network resilience compared to 
attributes-based and topological metrics. Performance-based 
metrics consider actual network performance indicators, such 
as travel time and demand satisfaction, which provide a more 
accurate reflection of resilience. In contrast, attributes-based 
metrics focus on network characteristics, such as 
infrastructure quantity, and topological metrics overlook 
network flows, making them less suitable for measuring 
resilience. Overall, performance-based metrics offer a more 
accurate and comprehensive assessment of transportation 
network resilience. 

B. Measurement Approaches 

To evaluate the performance of transportation systems and 
calculate resilience metrics, various measurement approaches 
have been employed. These performance evaluation methods 
can be classified into topological models, optimization models, 
simulation models, probability-based models, and data-driven 
models. 

Topological Models: These models play a crucial role in 
evaluating the performance of transportation systems by 
analyzing the structure, connectivity, and resilience of 
transportation networks. They consider factors such as 
network topology, node connectivity, link capacities, and the 
ability to withstand disruptions or failures. By examining 
these aspects, these models provide insights into the overall 
performance and resilience of transportation systems. Jani? 
(2019)[26] modeled the resilience of an airline cargo transport 
network by representing airports as nodes and air routes as 
links. This approach enabled the assessment of network 
performance and resilience in the face of disruptions. 
Sohouenou et al. (2020)[27] developed the GREREC model 


to generate abstract networks that resemble real-road 
networks in terms of their topological and operational 
characteristics. They conducted a robustness analysis on these 
generated networks. Xu et al. (2022)[28] conducted an 
empirical study on the resilience of five global metro systems 
using a graph theory and complex network approach. The 
analysis incorporated historical flow data to account for user 
behavior impacts. The study compared the resilience of the 
five metro networks and found that the Singapore metro 
exhibited the highest level of resilience. Tachaudomdach et al. 
(2021)[6] proposed quantifying robustness using edge- and 
node-betweenness centrality models. They assessed various 
factors such as the damaged area, affected links, affected 
nodes, and the number of affected cars to evaluate robustness 
under different flood levels. The measurement of when the 
system reaches a point where it cannot recover was also 
considered. Qi et al. (2022)[8] used complex network 
modeling to measure the topological characteristics of a metro 
system, focusing on node centralities to assess the importance 
of stations and their roles in the network. 

Optimization Models: Optimization models are 
instrumental during the design and operation phases to 
maximize resilience within budgetary and other constraints. 
These models aid in achieving optimal outcomes by 
identifying the most effective strategies and solutions. Liao et 
al. (2018)[11] proposed an optimization model to enhance 
transportation resilience under disasters by maximizing 
connectivity between origins and destinations while 
considering budget and traversal time constraints. Ren et al. 
(2019)[29] used a two-stage stochastic mixed-integer 
programming model to optimize the design of a compound 
public transport network (CPTN) consisting of urban rail 
transit (URT) and ground bus transit (GRT) stations. They 
proposed a network resilience index based on travel time as 
the objective function, aiming to improve the resilience of the 
CPTN by reducing the negative impact on passenger travel. 
Zhao and Zhang (2020)[30] proposed a bi-objective bi-level 
optimization framework to determine an optimal 
transportation infrastructure restoration plan. They considered 
unmet demand and total travel time as measures and used 
Elastic User Equilibrium to model the demand-supply 
imbalance. Kaviani et al. (2020)[20] proposed an optimization 
model for recovery planning of road networks to achieve 
social and economic resilience. The model was formulated as 
a bi-level multi-objective problem, aiming to maximize 
demand satisfaction and travel time resilience for both people 
and goods movement. Rahdar et al. (2022)[25] proposed a 
trilevel optimization model to improve transportation network 
resilience under uncertainty. The model minimized travel time 
by considering worst-case disruptions and expanding the 
network for enhanced resilience. Yin et al. (2023)[31] used a 
resilience-oriented train rescheduling framework for urban 
rail systems. They employed a mixed-integer linear 
programming (MILP) model with the objective of maximizing 
the resilience of the rail line against disruptions. Kurth et al. 
(2020)[66] calculated economic implications of unmitigated 
random disruptions in urban road systems.The model was 
applied to 10 cities in the United States to forecast the impact 
of disruptions on gross domestic product (GDP). They found 
that losses tend to be higher in wealthier, more economically 
productive cities. 

Simulation Models: Simulation is vital for assessing 
transportation network resilience, allowing evaluation under 
different scenarios. It helps detect vulnerabilities and weak 
performance areas. However, scalability and processing 
capacity pose limitations. Simulation can be applied to various 
transportation modes, considering their unique characteristics 
and interdependency. Wang et al. (2019)[19] simulated the 
operational performance of an air transport system under 
degraded capacity. They aimed to assess network disruption 
tolerance and identify critical airports impacting resilience. 
Nogal and Honfi (2019)[32] proposed a stochastic approach 
to assess the resilience of road transport networks. Their 
dynamic model considered the biased decisions of users based 
on their perception of traffic conditions. The approach 
provided more realistic patterns, especially in terms of 
recovery times. Duy et al. (2019)[33] used hydrological flood 
simulation and GIS analysis to assess the increasing 
vulnerability of Ho Chi Minh City's transport system to 
extreme floods and developed flood resilience strategies. 
Specking et al. (2019)[34] emphasized the importance of 
quantifying performance and using modeling and simulation 
to assess system resilience under specific missions and 
scenarios. They highlighted the need to consider system 
mission resilience, as it could improve performance in certain 
measures while potentially impacting others. Yao and Chen 
(2023)[35] developed a new modeling method for resilience. 
By integrating limited real-world data and microscopic traffic 
simulation, they conducted a comprehensive time-progressive 
resilience assessment of disrupted traffic systems at different 
spatial scales. 

Probability-based Models: Probability-based models 
emphasize quantifying the likelihood of failure and its 
consequences on network performance, serving as a reliable 
metric for assessing resilience. Reliability, as a key element of 
resilience, plays a vital role in proactive disaster preparedness 
efforts. These methods enable a better understanding of the 
potential risks and impacts on the network, contributing to 
effective resilience planning and mitigation strategies. 
Bayesian probabilistic networks use graphical representation 
and probability to predict network states based on element 
relationships and probabilities. Sharma and George (2018)[36] 
proposed a Bayesian-based model for assessing the resilience 
of a truckload transportation network. Their model 
incorporated the effectiveness probabilities of various 
measures to evaluate the network's resilience. Tang et al. 
(2019)[13] developed a Bayesian network model to assess the 
general resilience of Beijing's road transportation system from 
1997 to 2016. The results revealed a "V" shape trend in the 
general resilience of the road system. Drawing upon a light 
rail ridership dataset in Salt Lake County from 2017 to 2021, 
Xiao et al. (2022)[37] developed a novel method to measure 
the vulnerability and resilience of transit ridership using a 
Bayesian structure time series model. The results showed that 
government policies had a more significant impact than the 
number of COVID-19 cases on transit ridership. Wei et al. 
(2023)[17] used a Bayesian network and the FMEA method 
to assess the resilience of a subway system in real-time, 
considering multiple performance indicators and the impact of 
extreme weather. 

Data-Driven Models: Advancements in sensors, data 
storage, and processing have facilitated the generation of 
substantial amounts of raw data in transportation networks. 
These large-scale databases offer valuable information for 
assessing resilience. However, the effectiveness of the data 
relies on proper handling, filtering, and processing to extract 
relevant parameters. Big data methods aim to efficiently 
manage the overwhelming data flow while minimizing 


resource consumption, providing a method to represent the 
dynamic changes in transportation networks and assess their 
resilience. Ilbeigi (2019)[4] used taxi GPS traces to develop 
and quantify topological features of transportation networks. 
A process control was implemented to monitor network 
characteristics and analyze resilience, demonstrated through a 
case study of New York City's transportation network during 
Hurricane Sandy. Diab and Shalaby (2019)[38] conducted a 
study using statistical analysis and filtering of metro line 
service records to assess the resilience of the transit system in 
terms of "Lost Days of Service" caused by storms. They 
focused on the impact of outdoor track segments and weather 
conditions on service interruptions and delays at the stop level. 
Wang et al. (2020)[15] developed a framework that leveraged 
real-world big data, including on-demand ride services data 
and grid meteorological data, to capture the spatiotemporal 
dependencies of urban road networks and evaluated 
transportation resilience. Zhang et al. (2020)[21] used 
historical passenger flow data, real-time automatic fare 
collection system data, and capacity information to evaluate 
the resilience of urban rail transit passenger flow. Zhou et al. 
(2021)[16] assessed the resilience of metro ridership during 
extreme rainfall events using AFC data from the Shenzhen 
Metro. They analyzed the spatio-temporal characteristics of 
passenger flow and introduced meteorological warning 
signals and a ridership resilience curve. 

C. Enhancement of Resilience 

In the literature, the enhancement of resilience in 
transportation systems is often accompanied by the 
measurement of resilience to assess the effectiveness of 
enhancement strategies. This section discusses the strategies 
that have been adopted in the literature to enhance the 
resilience of transportation systems. 

One way to conceptualize the life cycle of a disaster is to 
divide it into four distinct phases: mitigation, preparedness, 
response, and recovery. It is noteworthy that all transportation 
system resilience-enhancing strategies are directed towards 
one or more of these phases. As summarized in Table?, the 
second column indicates the phase(s) to which the strategy is 
applicable, while the third column enumerates the specific 
measures employed. The table presents a comprehensive 
overview of the strategies used to improve transportation 
network resilience, with a focus on the phases that they target. 

Upon reviewing the literature, it becomes apparent that the 
majority of studies focus on a single phase of the disaster and 
propose corresponding strategies. Specifically, mitigation 
strategies aim to enhance the resilience of the transportation 
network by retrofitting its vulnerable components. However, 
in certain situations, such as when disasters are unavoidable 
or retrofitting costs are prohibitively high, preparedness 
measures taken prior to the onset of a disaster can greatly aid 
in post-disaster response and recovery efforts. This 
underscores the importance of taking a comprehensive and 
integrated approach to transportation network resilience.
